subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Quasi-Experimental Design and Reinforcement
stimulus:
Michael is the regional manager for a car company and needs to give a presentation to the president of the company about how different management styles might relate to the total number of sales of the company's new electric car. He hires an industrial/organizational psychologist to study three stores, each in a different city. Each store's manager has their own distinct management style:
• In Store A, the manager has a supportive style (open and warm).
• In Store B, the manager has a strict style (rigid and severe).
• In Store C, the manager, who is Michael's good friend, has a distant style (permissive and disengaged).
The number of electric cars sold at each store over a five-month period is shown in the table.
ELECTRIC CARS SOLD BY STORE
| Month | Store A (supportive style) | Store B (strict style) | Store C (distant style) |
|---|---|---|---|
| January | 9 | 7 | 3 |
| February | 7 | 8 | 2 |
| March | 9 | 5 | 5 |
| April | 5 | 3 | 4 |
| May | 5 | 2 | 1 |

parts.prompt: Explain why this study is not a true experiment.

scoring_points.description:
Response must indicate that this study is not an experiment because the researcher did NOT  use random assignment. 

OR 

Response must indicate that the study did not include the manipulation of a variable.

required_elements: The study is not a true experiment because the psychologist did not randomly assign participants to his groups. The study used stores that already existed instead of manipulating an independent variable. The independent variable was not manipulated by the researcher in this study.
alternatives.correct_example: The study is not a true experiment because the psychologist did not randomly assign participants to his groups. The study used stores that already existed instead of manipulating an independent variable. The independent variable was not manipulated by the researcher in this study.

wrong_examples: Michael didn't use random selection, so his study is not an experiment.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must identify either lack of random assignment OR lack of variable manipulation as the reason this is not a true experiment. Responses that cite random selection (sampling from a population) rather than random assignment (placing participants into conditions) do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The study is not a true experiment because the psychologist did not randomly assign participants to the groups — the stores and their management styles already existed.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the absence of random assignment as the disqualifying feature.

sample_responses.response: The study used stores that already existed instead of the researcher manipulating an independent variable.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable alternative — the researcher did not create or control the management style conditions; they were pre-existing.

sample_responses.response: Michael didn't use random selection, so his study is not an experiment.
sample_responses.commentary: Confuses random selection (sampling) with random assignment (assigning to conditions). Random selection is not the requirement for a true experiment.

parts.prompt: Identify the implied dependent variable for the study if the study had been a true experiment.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the number of cars sold would be the dependent variable.

required_elements: The DV would be the number of cars bought by customers. The DV is cars sold.
alternatives.correct_example: The DV would be the number of cars bought by customers. The DV is cars sold.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The dependent variable is the number of cars sold — the outcome measured across the three stores. Responses that identify the stores, the cars themselves, or the management/leadership style (which is the independent variable) do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The dependent variable would be the number of electric cars sold at each store.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the measured outcome (cars sold) as the dependent variable.

sample_responses.response: The DV is sales.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable shorthand — 'sales' clearly refers to the number of cars sold.

sample_responses.response: The DV is leadership style.
sample_responses.commentary: Leadership/management style is the independent variable (the condition being compared), not the dependent variable.

sample_responses.response: The DV would be the stores.
sample_responses.commentary: The stores are the units of measurement, not the variable being measured. The dependent variable is what is measured within each store.

parts.prompt: Calculate the mean of Store B's sales.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the mean for Store B is 5.

required_elements: Response must indicate that the mean for Store B is 5.

scoring_points.official_rubric: Store B monthly sales: 7 + 8 + 5 + 3 + 2 = 25. Mean = 25 / 5 = 5. The only acceptable answer is 5 (or five). Any other number does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The mean for Store B is 5. (7 + 8 + 5 + 3 + 2 = 25; 25 / 5 = 5)
sample_responses.commentary: Correct calculation: sum of Store B's monthly sales (25) divided by 5 months equals 5.

sample_responses.response: The mean is five.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — correct answer expressed in words.

sample_responses.response: The mean is 12.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect calculation. The sum 7+8+5+3+2 = 25, and 25/5 = 5, not 12.

parts.prompt: Explain why the location of the store is a confounding variable in this study.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the location of the store is affecting the dependent variable in  some specified way.

required_elements: Each management style was also in a different city, and people in each city may have different tastes in items and will purchase things differently. Cities with more people would most likely sell more units. Store A is in a higher-income area, so more cars are sold there.
alternatives.correct_example: Each management style was also in a different city, and people in each city may have different tastes in items and will purchase things differently. Cities with more people would most likely sell more units. Store A is in a higher-income area, so more cars are sold there.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must explain a specific mechanism by which city/location could affect the number of cars sold (the DV), making it impossible to know whether management style or location caused the difference in sales. Simply noting that stores are in different cities without explaining how this affects sales does not earn the point. Responses that describe access to the stores (e.g., 'people couldn't go to all stores') rather than factors affecting purchasing do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Each store is in a different city, and people in each city may have different buying power or preferences for electric cars — so the city itself could explain the difference in sales rather than the management style.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies city-level demographic or preference differences as a confound that could account for sales variation.

sample_responses.response: Cities with more people would most likely sell more units, so population size could be driving the differences in sales rather than management style.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — population size is a specific city characteristic that would affect sales independently of management style.

sample_responses.response: The location of the stores was confounding because some people couldn't go to all the stores.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not explain how location affects the dependent variable (number of cars sold). Customer access across stores is not the relevant confound.

parts.prompt: Explain how the halo effect could influence Michael's interpretation of the findings.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Michael's previous positive perception of Store C's manager  will cause him to think more positively toward Store C's sales data than he otherwise  would.

required_elements: Michael's good feeling about the manager at Store C will make him think the data for that store are wrong.
alternatives.correct_example: Michael's good feeling about the manager at Store C will make him think the data for that store are wrong.

required_elements: Michael is good friends with the manager of Store C, so that causes him to make a positive evaluation of the store's performance.
alternatives.correct_example: Michael is good friends with the manager of Store C, so that causes him to make a positive evaluation of the store's performance.

wrong_examples: Michael views the manager positively, but the data lead him to think he may have
scoring_points.official_rubric: The halo effect occurs when a positive impression of one characteristic (the friendship) leads to a more positive evaluation of other characteristics (the store's sales performance). Michael's friendship with Store C's manager should bias him toward a more positive interpretation of that store's data. Responses that show Michael reaching a negative conclusion about the manager despite the friendship do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Because Michael is good friends with Store C's manager, the halo effect will cause him to view that store's (poor) sales performance more positively than the data warrant — he may dismiss or rationalize the low numbers.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies the halo effect: positive overall impression of the manager causes Michael to evaluate Store C's data more favorably.

sample_responses.response: Michael is good friends with the manager of Store C, so that causes him to make a positive evaluation of the store's performance.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable CB example — the friendship (positive impression) spills over into a positive evaluation of the performance data.

sample_responses.response: Michael views the manager positively, but the data lead him to think he may have chosen a bad manager.
sample_responses.commentary: The halo effect would cause Michael to positively interpret the data despite the friendship — not to conclude the manager is bad. This response shows the data overriding the bias, which is the opposite of the halo effect.

parts.prompt: Explain why the hiring of an industrial/organizational psychologist was appropriate for this study.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that I/O psychologists are interested in optimizing or  improving performance OR solving problems in the workplace.

required_elements: This study is about which management style leads to the most sales, so an I/O psychologist would be best to help solve this problem.
alternatives.correct_example: This study is about which management style leads to the most sales, so an I/O psychologist would be best to help solve this problem.

required_elements: Michael wants to identify ways to improve managers' performance, and an I/O psychologist can help him with this goal.
alternatives.correct_example: Michael wants to identify ways to improve managers' performance, and an I/O psychologist can help him with this goal.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must connect I/O psychology's core function — optimizing workplace performance or solving organizational problems — to the study's goal of understanding management style and sales. Responses that state only 'I/O psychologists study the workplace' without explaining their specific function (improving/optimizing performance) do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: This study is about which management style leads to the most sales, so an I/O psychologist is appropriate because they specialize in optimizing performance and solving problems in organizational settings.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the I/O psychologist's function (optimizing performance) and connects it to the study's goal.

sample_responses.response: Michael wants to identify ways to improve his managers' performance, and an I/O psychologist is trained specifically to help improve workplace performance.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — directly links the I/O psychologist's expertise to the organizational goal of improving performance.

sample_responses.response: I/O psychologists study the workplace.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague. Does not specify what I/O psychologists do in the workplace (optimize performance, solve organizational problems) that makes them appropriate for this study.

parts.prompt: Explain how Michael could use positive reinforcement to help him finish his presentation by the deadline.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate Michael could reward himself with something that he likes each  time he works on his presentation to make it more likely that he will keep working.

required_elements: Michael treats himself to a small piece of candy after he works for 20 minutes to encourage himself to keep working.
alternatives.correct_example: Michael treats himself to a small piece of candy after he works for 20 minutes to encourage himself to keep working.

wrong_examples: Michael is positive with himself about finishing the report on time.
Michael treats himself to candy after working on his presentation.
Michael eats a piece of candy and then begins to work on his presentation.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Positive reinforcement requires: (1) a rewarding stimulus presented AFTER the behavior and (2) the reward increasing the likelihood of the behavior recurring. The behavior must occur BEFORE the reward. Responses that describe the reward coming before the behavior (eat candy then work) do not earn the point. Responses that describe self-encouragement without a concrete reward do not earn the point. Responses that omit the contingency between the behavior and the reward do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Michael treats himself to a small piece of candy after he works for 20 minutes on his presentation — this positive reinforcement increases the likelihood that he will continue working.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies positive reinforcement: reward (candy) follows the behavior (working), making the behavior more likely to recur.

sample_responses.response: Michael is positive with himself about finishing the report on time.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes positive attitude, not positive reinforcement. No concrete rewarding stimulus is presented after the behavior.

sample_responses.response: Michael treats himself to candy after working on his presentation.
sample_responses.commentary: Close but insufficient — does not specify that the reward follows a defined behavior (e.g., working for a set time) or that it increases the likelihood of the behavior recurring. The contingency is unclear.

sample_responses.response: Michael eats a piece of candy and then begins to work on his presentation.
sample_responses.commentary: The reward comes BEFORE the behavior, which is bribery, not reinforcement. Positive reinforcement requires the reward to follow the behavior.
